3 min read
2 hours ago

--

Three talks that move past “AI writes my code” and into building real systems in JavaScript.
Most of the AI conversation in web development has settled into a comfortable groove: the editor autocompletes, you accept the suggestion, you move on. It’s useful, but it’s a small slice of what’s actually changing. The AI sessions at JS Days 2026 are interesting precisely because they skip the autocomplete story and go straight to the harder question: what happens when AI stops suggesting lines and starts assembling working systems that you steer?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Quick disclosure before we dig in: I’m on the team at Sencha that helps run JS Days, so read me as an enthusiastic insider. But these three talks are the ones I’d point a skeptical friend toward, because they are about shipping, not slideware. Here is what each one covers and why it matters. (JS Days 2026 is free and online, running September 16–17.)
1. Building custom AI agents in JavaScript with React (Marc Gusmano)
What you’ll learn: how to build an AI agent inside a real JavaScript and React application using ReExt (React plus Ext JS components). Not a chatbot bolted onto the corner of the screen, but an agent that takes a goal, calls your app’s functions, checks the results, and iterates, while you hold the guardrails.
Why it matters: the shift from autocomplete to agents changes how you architect an app. You have to think about exposing your app’s actions as tools the agent can call, about permissions and sandboxing, about where the agent’s state lives, and about what happens when it gets something wrong. These are new design problems for most front-end developers, and getting to watch someone build through them, rather than describe them on a slide, is the fastest way to learn the shape of the work.
2. AI-powered UI components (Andres Villalba)
What you’ll learn: where AI actually fits into the front end beyond the chat box. This session looks at UI components that adapt, assist, or generate, and how to build them without the user experience quietly falling apart.
Why it matters: the interface is where AI meets the person using your software, and it is also where it most often goes wrong. An AI feature that is powerful but unpredictable in the UI erodes trust fast. Learning to build components that use AI while staying legible and controllable is quickly becoming a core front-end skill rather than a novelty, and this is a practical look at doing it well.
3. Generating apps on top of an existing database with AI (Pavel Perminov)
What you’ll learn: how to use AI, in this case Indi Engine, to generate Ext JS applications on top of an existing database, turning a schema into a working application scaffold instead of hand-building it screen by screen.
Why it matters: a huge share of real enterprise work is essentially structured views over a database, the forms, grids, and dashboards that sit on top of your data. If AI can scaffold that layer reliably, it changes the economics of internal tools and line-of-business apps. This is one of the more concrete examples of AI doing meaningful application development rather than just answering questions, and it is worth seeing where it holds up and where a human still has to step in.
The thread running through all three
Put the three together and a pattern emerges. Each one is really about the same shift: AI moving from writing snippets to assembling and operating systems, with the developer’s job moving from typing code to designing, constraining, and steering. That is a more interesting and more durable change than “it finishes my functions for me,” and it raises real questions about architecture, trust, and where the human stays in the loop.
Whether you are excited about that or wary of it, these are the questions worth engaging with now rather than later, and this is a good, low-cost way to do it.
Where and when
JS Days 2026 is free and online, September 16–17. You can register here: jsdays.io.
